one_line_takeaway: Text-DJ shows that rendering a decomposed harmful query as text-in-image tiles scattered among semantically unrelated distraction-query images reliably jailbreaks state-of-the-art open- and closed-source LVLMs (Qwen3-VL, GPT-4o-mini/4.1-mini, Gemini-2.5-Flash) via their OCR pathway, beating prior image-based jailbreak baselines (HADES, CS-DJ) while remaining largely undetected by both the OpenAI Moderation API and GuardReasoner-VL guard models.
key_finding: On the HADES benchmark, Text-DJ's average ASR exceeds both baselines on every victim model tested: e.g. Qwen3-VL-4B 48.13% (Text-DJ) vs 34.40% (CS-DJ) vs 11.87% (HADES); Qwen3-VL-8B 52.67% vs 38.53% vs 9.33%; GPT-4o-mini 59.33% vs 46.80% vs 4.13%; GPT-4.1-mini 64.27% vs 56.00% vs 8.40%; Gemini-2.5-Flash 44.00% vs 40.53% vs 21.33% (Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B was the one case where Text-DJ, 57.60%, only marginally beat CS-DJ's 56.27%). On HEx-PHI, Text-DJ again outperforms CS-DJ across all six victim models, e.g. GPT-4o-mini 59.00% vs 44.67%, GPT-4.1-mini 60.33% vs 45.00%, Qwen3-VL-8B 47.33% vs 34.33%. Under guard-model defenses (Table 3, HADES/Qwen3-VL), the OpenAI Moderation API produced 0.00% ASR reduction across all categories and models (it flagged none of the Text-DJ inputs), while GuardReasoner-VL-3B/7B reduced ASR only modestly (e.g. Qwen3-VL-8B average ASR dropped from 52.67% to 48.93% under GuardReasoner-VL-3B and to 43.47% under GuardReasoner-VL-7B), i.e. the attack remains highly effective even under strong input-level filtering. Ablations show: (1) removing TiI (i.e., giving the same decomposed/distraction text directly instead of as images) sharply lowers ASR (e.g. Qwen3-VL-8B average drops from 52.67% to 11.60%), confirming the cross-modal/OCR pathway -- not merely query decomposition -- drives the attack; (2) selecting maximally-unrelated distractors beats random distractor selection; (3) sentence-embedding-based distractor selection is comparably effective to, and slightly better and simpler than, CLIP image-embedding-based selection; (4) decomposition is necessary -- m=1 (no decomposition) consistently fails, and m=3 was chosen as the best tradeoff; (5) ASR rises with more distraction queries n but with diminishing returns, with a 'sweet spot' around n=9-12; (6) placing sub-queries in later-but-not-last grid positions maximizes ASR; (7) randomized WCAG-AA colorization outperforms a fixed red-on-white scheme.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Text-DJ (Text Distraction Jailbreaking), a black-box, model-agnostic jailbreak attack against Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) that relies exclusively on textual prompts rendered as images. The method has three stages: (1) an LLM (prompted Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct) decomposes a single harmful query q into m semantically related but individually more benign sub-queries; (2) a greedy diverse-subset-selection algorithm (using cosine similarity over sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings) picks n 'distraction queries' from an offline pool of benign science/culture questions that are maximally dissimilar to the harmful query and to each other; (3) all m+n queries are rendered as text-in-image (TiI) tiles with randomized WCAG-AA-compliant (contrast >= 4.5) text/background colors, arranged into a grid (m+n=12 images in a 4x3 grid in the main experiments), with the sub-queries placed at specific non-edge grid positions (indices 6, 8, 12), and the composed grid image plus an instruction prompt is sent to the victim LVLM, which uses its OCR/visual-reading capability to reconstruct and answer the harmful query. The authors argue the attack succeeds via two mechanisms: (a) converting text to images bypasses text-based safety filters, and (b) scattering benign-looking sub-queries among many irrelevant 'distraction' queries prevents the model's safety alignment from re-assembling and recognizing the harmful intent ('semantic distraction' rather than the 'visual distraction' used by prior work).
threat_model: Black-box attacker with only query access (no gradients or model parameters) to a deployed LVLM (open- or closed-source), who submits multimodal inputs (an image grid plus text instruction) and observes the model's textual output. The attacker does not need image-generation/diffusion capability (unlike HADES) or real-world distractor images (unlike CS-DJ); the only asset needed is a decomposer LLM, a sentence-embedding model, a pool of benign distractor questions, and a text-to-image rendering routine. The attack also explicitly targets deployments guarded by an external input-level safety filter (OpenAI Moderation API, GuardReasoner-VL-3B/7B) placed in front of the LVLM.
limitations: The paper's own text notes: HADES was excluded from HEx-PHI evaluation due to its complexity to generate adversarial images and 'significant under-performance' on the HADES dataset itself, limiting baseline breadth on that dataset. The distraction-query pool is restricted to randomly generated science/culture queries (not systematically varied by domain). Ablations were conducted only on open-source Qwen3-VL models 'for reproducibility,' so ablation conclusions (e.g., optimal n, m, position, colorization) are not independently verified on the closed-source victims (GPT-4o-mini/4.1-mini, Gemini-2.5-Flash) used in the main results. Safety judgments rely on a single LLM-as-a-judge model (beaver-dam-7b), introducing potential judge-model bias/noise not cross-validated against a second judge or human annotation. The paper does not evaluate or propose a concrete defense against the attack beyond noting that OCR-module safety alignment is 'largely overlooked' and calling for future work on 'more robust defenses for the OCR modules.'

datasets_used: HADES (750 harmful prompts; 5 categories: Animal, Financial, Privacy, Self-Harm, Violence; 150 each)
HEx-PHI (300 harmful queries; 10 categories after removal of one category per the dataset's Aug 2024 revision, 30 queries each)
Offline distraction-query pool: randomly generated science/culture queries (author-constructed, listed in appendix)
quantitative_results: HADES avg ASR, Qwen3-VL-4B: Text-DJ 48.13% vs CS-DJ 34.40% vs HADES 11.87%
HADES avg ASR, Qwen3-VL-8B: Text-DJ 52.67% vs CS-DJ 38.53% vs HADES 9.33%
HADES avg ASR, Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B: Text-DJ 57.60% vs CS-DJ 56.27% vs HADES 13.60%
HADES avg ASR, GPT-4o-mini: Text-DJ 59.33% vs CS-DJ 46.80% vs HADES 4.13%
HADES avg ASR, GPT-4.1-mini: Text-DJ 64.27% vs CS-DJ 56.00% vs HADES 8.40%
HADES avg ASR, Gemini-2.5-Flash: Text-DJ 44.00% vs CS-DJ 40.53% vs HADES 21.33%
HEx-PHI avg ASR, GPT-4o-mini: Text-DJ 59.00% vs CS-DJ 44.67%
HEx-PHI avg ASR, GPT-4.1-mini: Text-DJ 60.33% vs CS-DJ 45.00%
HEx-PHI avg ASR, Gemini-2.5-Flash: Text-DJ 41.33% vs CS-DJ 41.00%
Defense (HADES, Qwen3-VL-8B): No Defense avg ASR 52.67% -> OpenAI Moderation API 52.67% (-0.00) -> GuardReasoner-VL-3B 48.93% (-3.73) -> GuardReasoner-VL-7B 43.47% (-9.20)
OpenAI Moderation API refusal rate on Text-DJ inputs (HADES, all victim models/categories): 0.00% throughout
Ablation - removing TiI (text-to-image step), Qwen3-VL-8B avg ASR: 52.67% (with TiI) vs 11.60% (without TiI)
Ablation - m (sub-queries): m=1 (no decomposition) consistently fails; m=3 selected as best tradeoff
Ablation - n (distraction queries) tested at 0,3,6,9,12,15; ASR rises with n with diminishing returns, sweet spot n=9-12
EASR (5-run ensemble, HADES, appendix Table 5), Qwen3-VL-8B: Text-DJ 82.13% vs CS-DJ 73.87%
baselines_compared: HADES (diffusion-generated harmful images; Li et al., ECCV 2024)
CS-DJ / 'Distraction is all you need' (real-world images as visual distraction; Yang et al., CVPR 2025)
